mappings.rationaleAI: Folk religion with its festivals and rites of passage connects to religious feasts and festivals (A.4). Oral traditions and folk beliefs as expressive culture relate to sacred writings transmitted informally (C.1).

Ritual as formalized symbolic action directly addresses religious rites and customs (A). Rites of passage, calendrical rites, and sacrifice connect to passage and purification rites (A.2), rituals of worship (A.1), and religious feasts and festivals (A.4).
Confucius's emphasis on ritual propriety (li) and his role in establishing an educational tradition that shaped Chinese statecraft connect to institutions of religious education (B.4).

The Catholic Church's hierarchical structure of bishops, cardinals, and the Holy See exemplifies forms of religious organization (B.2). Apostolic succession and sainthood connect to sainthood (B.3). Its art, architecture, and philosophy relate to art and architecture, religious symbolism (C.2). The sacraments are ceremonial and religious objects (C.3).

Jesus as central figure of Christianity, whose apostles established religious institutions, relates to religious leaders. His canonization as object of worship connects to sainthood (B.3) in the broader sense of exemplary religious figures.

Muhammad's combination of prophetic, legislative, and state-building roles established a model of theocratic governance (B.1). His establishment of Islamic law through the Quran and hadith connects to religious law (A.3.a) and forms of religious organization (B.2).

Luther's challenge to papal authority and establishment of the priesthood of all believers transformed forms of religious organization (B.2). His translation of the Bible into vernacular German relates to sacred writings (C.1).
Prayer as communicative act encompassing supplication, intercession, thanksgiving, and adoration directly constitutes rituals of worship (A.1) and prayer specifically (A.1.a).
Festivals rooted in religious observance — patronal commemorations, thanksgiving rituals, and seasonal celebrations — directly constitute religious feasts and festivals (A.4).
Oral transmission of religious canon including Vedic recitation, Islamic hadith, and African griot practice directly relates to sacred writings and their oral transmission (C.1).

Religion encompassing organized systems of beliefs, practices, sacred texts, and institutions broadly covers religious rites and customs (A), religious leaders and institutions (B), and material manifestations of religious beliefs (C).

Culture's social behaviors, norms, and customs relate to religious regulation of personal and social behaviour (A.3). Cultural expression through art and symbolism connects to art and architecture, religious symbolism (C.2).
The historical development of religious rituals, institutions, and beliefs cross-culturally provides context for religious rites and customs (A) and religious leaders and institutions (B).
Hammurabi's law code, legitimated by religious authority and governing social conduct, connects to religious law (A.3.a). His role as divinely sanctioned king relates to sacred kingships (B.1).
Ramesses II as divine pharaoh exemplifies sacred kingship (B.1). His monumental temple construction at Abu Simbel and the Ramesseum represents art and architecture with religious symbolism (C.2).

Ashoka's reorientation of governance around dhamma after converting to Buddhism connects to theocracies and sacred kingships (B.1). His patronage of the Third Buddhist Council and sending of missionaries relates to institutions of religious education (B.4).
Joan of Arc's canonization by the Catholic Church in 1920 directly relates to sainthood (B.3). Her claimed divine visions connect to prophecy and divination (A.3.f).

Gandhi's Hindu ethics, ascetic self-discipline, and satyagraha (nonviolent resistance) grounded in religious principles connect to religious regulation of personal and social behaviour (A.3) and asceticism (A.3.e).

Confucianism's emphasis on ritual propriety (li) and hierarchical social relationships connects to religious regulation of personal and social behaviour (A.3). Its shaping of education across the Sinosphere relates to institutions of religious education (B.4).
Religious dress codes and culturally specific garments worn for religious purposes directly address religious dress and vestments (C.4).

Cyrus's policy of religious tolerance and the Edict of Restoration freeing Jewish exiles connect to the religious state and sacred kingship as governance forms intertwined with religious authority (B.1).

Suleiman's synthesis of sultanic and Sharia legal codes connects to religious law (A.3.a). His role as caliph-sultan represents theocratic governance (B.1). His patronage of Ottoman architecture connects to art and architecture with religious dimensions (C.2).
Marriage regulated by religious doctrine and tribal custom, with forms varying across traditions, directly relates to passage rites including marriage (A.2).

Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling, Last Judgment, Pieta, and St. Peter's dome represent the highest achievements in religious art and architecture, directly addressing art and architecture, religious symbolism and iconography (C.2).

Aquinas as a Dominican friar whose theological synthesis shaped Catholic institutional teaching connects to the priesthood and forms of religious organization (B.2). His role in scholasticism relates to institutions of religious education (B.4).

Divination practices connect to prophecy and divination (A.3.f). Festivals as vehicles of transmission relate to religious feasts and festivals (A.4). Oral rather than scriptural transmission connects to sacred writings and their oral forms (C.1).
The Quran as Islam's central scripture, the primary source of Islamic law alongside hadith, directly constitutes sacred writings (C.1).

Jerusalem's Old City with the Western Wall, Dome of the Rock, Church of the Holy Sepulchre, and al-Aqsa Mosque represents art and architecture with religious symbolism (C.2) and ceremonial and religious objects (C.3).
The Bhagavad Gita as Hinduism's most widely influential scripture is a foundational sacred writing (C.1).
Bach's sacred cantatas, Passions, and oratorios composed within the German Lutheran tradition represent material manifestations of religious beliefs through art, specifically religious music (C.2).

The Tale of Genji's Buddhist themes of impermanence and mono no aware represent cultural expressions of religious beliefs in literature, connecting to art and architecture as material manifestations (C.2).
